steps.text: Identify the chemical composition of the dust to determine if it could be combustible.
Go to the IHS Website for the latest NFPA standards.
Review Combustible Dust Equipment Considerations, Housekeeping Considerations, and Electrical (Hazard) Class II Considerations before beginning the inspections.
Develop a basic understanding of the industrial processes at the facility and of the associated dust equipment.
Ask questions early in the inspection, utilizing Appendix C (Common Inspection Questions) as assistance.
Review process-related documents, including P&IDs, process flow diagrams, process narratives, equipment description manuals, operating procedures, manufacturer documentation, and maintenance records.
Request information from the employer regarding the electrically classified locations at the facility.
Review the provided electrical classification information.
Inspect the classified locations in the facility to ensure they are properly classified and boundary distances are adequately defined.
Ensure that electrical equipment located in these areas is intrinsically safe, approved, or safe for the locations.
Assess that the powered industrial trucks used in those hazardous (classified) locations are appropriate.
Perform a dust hazard analysis (DHA)
Review Appendix C, Common Inspection Questions section of this chapter.
Confirm information about the hazards and characteristics of the combustible dust(s).
Ask the employer for drawings and other applicable documents to assist in walk-arounds.
Discuss the on-site combustible dust generating processes and associated equipment with the employer.
Discuss PPE and area entry requirements with the employer.
Wear the proper PPE into the field.
Document conditions in the field during the initial walk-around.
Take dust accumulation measurements.
Review applicable OSHA standard requirements and/or industry recognition of hazards and methods of abatement.
Communicate any documented hazards to the employer.
Collect the samples as low-MIE dusts.
Contact your supervisor for direction.
Contact RO, NO, and HRT experts for assistance.
Orient vent panel(s) so that they vent away from locations occupied by personnel, the building structure, and air intakes.
Calculate fireball dimensions per NFPA 68 guidance.
Implement additional engineering and administrative controls to ensure employee safety.
Design a ducting system to vent to a safe outdoor location.
Utilize a listed flame-arresting and dust-retention device for indoor venting.
Determine particulate parameters, including maximum pressure (Pmax) and deflagration index (KSt).
Design, install, and maintain venting systems using the manufacturer's recommendations.
Design, install, and maintain fast-acting mechanical valves using the manufacturer’s recommendations.
Design, install, and maintain actuated pinch valves using the manufacturer’s recommendations.

preconditions.text: If Combustible Dust Equipment Considerations are performed in conjunction with Housekeeping Considerations and Electrical (Hazard) Class II Considerations.
Before proceeding with the combustible dust electrical equipment inspection
Once classified locations in the facility have been identified
Where hazardous (classified) locations (i.e. Class II locations) have been documented
There is a potential combustible dust at a facility
CSHOs must understand the hazards and characteristics of the particular dust(s) at the site before proceeding.
CSHOs suspect that the dusts to be sampled may have MIEs at or below 30 mJ.
Dust does not meet the criteria in the categories but is observed to be an extremely fine powder (i.e., the size of common soft-white wheat flour or smaller).
CSHOs suspect that the dusts to be sampled may have other hazards.
Questions arise regarding dusts with other hazards.

The following sampling tools shall be available to CSHOs when performing low-MIE combustible dust inspections: 1-liter plastic sampling containers, Aluminum, conductive, non-sparking scoops, Natural bristle brushes, Sample seals (Form OSHA-21), and Bonding and grounding equipment (double hand clamp bonding wire, scoop bonding wire, and two heel grounders).
Collector is near an exterior wall

constraints.text: Chemicals and compounds react with oxygen at varying rates and burn with varying energies.
NFPA 33 should not be used to address combustible dust hazards at powder coating operations. 29 CFR 1910.107 is the vertical standard applicable at powder coating operations.
Hazardous (classified) locations must be properly documented in accordance with 29 CFR 1910.307(b).
Electrical equipment installed in hazardous locations must be intrinsically safe, approved, or safe for the locations as required by 29 CFR 1910.307(c).
If there is a question about the OSHA requirements for electrical classifications and equipment, or for powered industrial truck certifications, contact your supervisor.
A competent professional, experienced in the identification, evaluation, abatement, and control of combustible dust hazards, is crucial to conducting an effective DHA

PPE must include a hard hat, safety glasses, and FRC. Respirators may also be required, but only if the CSHO is medically approved and fit-tested for respirator use. Additional PPE may be necessary when low-MIE combustible dusts are encountered.
Self-heating and self-reactive dusts do not have to be dispersed in the air to ignite.
Dangerous-when-wet dusts react with water/moisture to release hydrogen gas. These dusts do not have to be dispersed in air to ignite.
Two (2) heel grounders should be used only while the low-MIE combustible dust sample is being taken.

Static dissipative footwear will not reliably provide adequate personnel grounding because floor deposits, residues, and coatings can limit floor conductivity and limit the effectiveness of such footwear.
Venting indoors with standard vent panels is considered hazardous.
The design of the system must be based on the worst case scenario properties of the particulate representative within the vessel.
The design of the system should be based on the worst case scenario properties of the particulate representative within the vessel at the facility’s process.
